questions.question:
Throughout the excerpt, Sal watches the Winterbottom family interact and then reflects on what matters, ‘in the course of a lifetime.’

According to the last paragraph, how does watching the Winterbottom family help Sal understand the difference between things that matter and things that don't matter in life? Use evidence from the text to support your response.

responses.content: In the course of a lifetime, what does it matter? I said it over and over I did not think cheerleading tryouts would matter, but I was not so sure about yelling at your mother. I was certain, however, that if your mother left, it would be something that mattered in the whole long .

Phoebe and I had seen Mr. Birkway and Mrs. Cadaver whacking away at the rhododendron, I walked home with Phoebe after school. She was as crotchety and sullen as a three-legged mule, and I was not quite sure why. She had been asking me why I had not said anything to my father about Mrs. Cadaver and Mr. Birkway, and I told her that I was waiting for the right time.
It surprised me when she said that, reminding me that I had told Phoebe nothing about my mother. "Yes, I suppose I would go live with her." That was impossible and I knew it, but for some reason I could not tell Phoebe that, so I lied
I am, I am, I am!" Phoebe shouted at her mother. "You don't have to bake things for me. I'm too fat. And you don't have to wait here for me to come home. I'm thirteen now."

Phoebe marched upstairs. Mrs. Winterbottom offered me a brownie, so I sat down at the table. What I started doing was remembering the day before my mother left. I did not know it was to be her last day home. Several times that day, my mother asked me if I wanted to walk up in the fields with her. It was drizzling outside, and I was cleaning out my desk, and I just did not feel like going. "Maybe later," I kept saying. When she asked me for about the tenth time, I said, "No! I don't want to go. Why do you keep asking me?" I don't know why I did that. I didn't mean anything by it, but that was one of the last memories she had of me, and I wished I could take it back.
(22) Her mother said, "Maybe I'll come along and watch." I could tell that Mrs. Winterbottom was trying to rise above some awful sadness she was feeling, but Prudence couldn't see that. Prudence had her own agenda, just as I had had my own agenda that day my mother wanted me to walk with her. I couldn't see my own mother's sadness
I heard the front door open and shut and Phoebe came in the kitchen waving a white envelope. "Guess what was on the steps?" she said.
 Mrs. Winterbottom took the envelope and turned it over and over before she slowly unsealed it and slipped out the message.
Oh," she said. "Who is doing this?" She held out the piece of paper: In the course of a lifetime, what does it matter?
Prudence said, "Well, I have more important things to worry about, I can assure you. I know I'm going to blow those cheerleading tryouts, I just know it.
On and on she went, until Phoebe said, "Cripes, Prudence, in the course of a lifetime, what does it matter?"
At that moment, it was if a switch went off in Mrs. Winterbottom's brain. She put her hand to her mouth and stared out the window. She was invisible to Prudence and Phoebe, though. They did not notice
As I walked home, I thought about the message. In the course of a lifetime, what does it matter? I said it over and over. I wondered about the mysterious messenger, and I wondered about all the things in the course of a lifetime that would not matter. I did not think cheerleading tryouts would matter, but I was not so sure about yelling at your mother. I was certain, however, that if your mother left, it would be something that mattered in the whole long course of your lifetime.
Em

questions.question:
Reread the following sentence from paragraph 15:


“We visited the Lok Virsa museum, and it was a joy to celebrate our national heritage once again.”


What does Malala's use of the phrase “once again” reveal about Swat's past?

responses.content: Islamabad was a beautiful place with nice white bungalows and broad roads, though it has none of the natural beauty of Swat
responses.bucket_rationale: The student's highlight, "Islamabad was a beautiful place with nice white bungalows and broad roads, though it has none of the natural beauty of Swat," is within the character limit and directly answers the question by providing a visual detail that helps readers picture what Islamabad looks like. The description of "nice white bungalows and broad roads" is a valid and specific visual detail from the text. The extra phrase about Swat does not make the answer too long or off-topic, so the response is correct.

questions.question: In paragraph 14, Malala compares Islamabad and Swat. What does this comparison reveal about her point of view on both cities?

responses.content: That it can happen again
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'That it can happen again' is generic and does not show understanding of the specific meaning of 'once again' in the context of Swat's past. The phrase 'once again' in the text refers to being able to celebrate national heritage as they did before, which was lost when the museum in Swat closed. The response does not reference Swat, the museum, or the idea of something being lost and then restored. It misreads 'once again' as simply repeating an event, not as regaining something that was lost. Therefore, it is classified as incorrect.

responses.content: That it happened more than once
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'That it happened more than once' shows some understanding that 'once again' refers to something being repeated, but it does not specify what was repeated or connect it to celebrating national heritage in Swat before the museum closed. The answer is text-specific and not generic, but it lacks the detail needed for a 'correct' classification, as it does not mention the loss or change in Swat's ability to celebrate heritage. Therefore, it fits best in the 'partial-understanding' bucket.

responses.content: They used to have a museum but now it is closed once again
responses.bucket_rationale: The response clearly indicates that Swat used to have a museum but now it is closed, which aligns with the key scene elements and the meaning of 'once again' in the text. The answer demonstrates understanding that there was a change in Swat's ability to celebrate its heritage, meeting both the form and accuracy requirements for the 'correct' bucket.

The answer is vague and does not fully capture Malala's point of view about both cities as described in the text. Therefore, it fits the 'partial-understanding' category.

However, the answer does not fully explain what the comparison reveals about Malala's feelings or perspective on both cities, nor does it mention what Malala appreciates about Islamabad (such as the nice white bungalows, broad roads, or modern features). The response is accurate but incomplete, fitting the 'partial-understanding' category.

questions.question: Highlight one detail from paragraph 13 that shows how media attention helped Malala's cause.

responses.content: But my grandmother wasn’t so sure. Whenever my grandmother saw me speaking on television, or leaving the house, she would pray, “Please God make Malala like Benazir Bhutto but do not give her Benazir’s short life.”
responses.bucket_rationale: The student's highlight is: 'But my grandmother wasn’t so sure. Whenever my grandmother saw me speaking on television, or leaving the house, she would pray, “Please God make Malala like Benazir Bhutto but do not give her Benazir’s short life.”' This is within the character limit and directly answers the question by showing how media attention (speaking on television) made Malala's grandmother worry for her safety, which is evidence of danger from media attention. The highlight is also an exact match to one of the accepted answers.

questions.question: Brainstorm 2-3 impacts the media had on Malala. You can write a list, use complete sentences, or use whatever format helps you think best.

responses.content: saw then the power of the media and she became a great support to us. My father was almost bursting with pride at how I came across in the documentary
responses.bucket_rationale:
Step 0: The only highlight to evaluate is: 'saw then the power of the media and she became a great support to us. My father was almost bursting with pride at how I came across in the documentary'.

Step 0A: The longest acceptable answer is: 'Her name was Shiza Shahid and she came from Islamabad. She had seen the New York Times documentary Class Dismissed in Swat Valley and tracked us down. We saw then the power of the media and she became a great support to us.' which is 176 characters. 1.3 x 176 = 228.8, 176 + 50 = 226. So, character limit is 229.

Step 0B: The student's highlight, after normalization, is 120 characters.

Step 0C: 120 < 229, so the highlight is within the limit.

Question analysis: The question asks for a detail from paragraph 13 that shows how media attention helped Malala's cause. The highlight 'saw then the power of the media and she became a great support to us' directly addresses this, showing that media attention brought support to Malala.

The second sentence about her father's pride is not required, but it does not make the highlight too long or off-topic. The main idea is present and clear.

Therefore, the highlight is correct.
